query: What insects come out after the rains in Southern Spain?

response.answer:
In Southern Spain, after the rains, several insect species may become more active or noticeable due to the increased moisture and availability of food sources. Here are some common groups that you might observe:

1. **Ants**: Rain can bring out ants as they forage for food and water. Species like black garden ants (Lasius niger) and red harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex sp.) may be more active.

2. **Beetles**: Various beetle species, including ground beetles (Carabidae), rove beetles (Staphylinidae), and darkling beetles (Tenebrionidae), can become more visible after rain as they seek out damp areas or prey on other insects that have emerged.

3. **Dragonflies and Damselflies**: After heavy rains, these aquatic insects may be seen flying around newly formed puddles or temporary ponds. Species like the common darter (Sympetrum striolatum) and various species of libellulids are commonly observed.

4. **Butterflies and Moths**: Some butterfly and moth species, particularly those that lay eggs on plants that thrive in wet conditions, may become more active. Examples include certain species of fritillary butterflies (Boloria sp.) and noctuid moths.

5. **Flies**: Rain can attract various fly species, including hoverflies (Syrphidae) which are often seen visiting flowers for nectar or searching for prey in damp areas.

6. **Spiders**: Some spider species may be more active after rain as they seek out new hunting grounds or mate. Orb-weaver spiders and ground-dwelling spiders can become more visible.

7. **Bees**: While bees might not be the first to come to mind, some bee species, especially those that forage on wetland plants, may become more noticeable after rainfall.

8. **Water Beetles**: Species of diving beetles (Dytiscidae) and water scavenger beetles (Hydrophilidae) can be seen in temporary pools or puddles formed by rain.

9. **Mosquitoes**: While not always desirable, mosquitoes may increase in numbers after heavy rains due to the availability of breeding sites in standing water.

10. **Springtails**: These tiny insects are often overlooked but can become more active after moisture, especially in damp soil and leaf litter.

These observations can vary depending on local conditions and specific microclimates within Southern Spain. If you're interested in observing these insects, it's best to go out during or shortly after rain when they are most likely to be active.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Pristimantis mutabilis GUAYASAMIN, KRYNAK, KRYNAK, CULEBRAS, & HUTTER 2015, SP. NOV.
treatments.articleTitle: Phenotypic plasticity raises questions for taxonomically important traits: a remarkable new Andean rainfrog (Pristimantis) with the ability to change skin texture
treatments.articleAuthor: Guayasamin, Juan M., Krynak, Tim, Krynak, Katherine, Culebras, Jaime & Hutter, Carl R.
treatments.journalTitle: Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society

images.captionText: Figure 1. (A, B, Pristimantis mutabilis sp. nov., holotype, MZUTI 2190, in preservative. C, D, Pristimantis verecundus, holotype, IND-AN 1834, in preservative.

Figure 3. Pristimantis mutabilis sp. nov. in life. A, B, Sub-adult male, MZUTI 2191, photographed in its natural habitat during the night (top left) and under laboratory conditions during the day (top right). C, Adult female in dorsolateral view, MZUTI 910. D, Adult female in ventral view, MZUTI 911.

Figure 4. Maximum likelihood (ML) and Bayesian (BA) genetic topology for the combined 12S and 16S dataset. Separate gene trees not shown because the results were similar. The circles at the nodes show ML and BA significant support values (ML: bootstrap> 75%; BA: posterior probability> 0.95). The lack of a circle indicates that the node was not significantly supported by either analysis. The (1) and (2) represent two possible evolutionary scenarios given current data. Scenario 1 hypothesizes that skin texture plasticity originated independently twice while Scenario 2 hypothesizes that skin plasticity originated once in the common ancestor to the P. myersi and P. surdus groups.

Figure 2. Skin texture variation in one individual frog (Pristimantis mutabilis) from Reserva Las Gralarias (Pichincha, Ecuador). Note that skin texture shifts from highly tubercular to almost smooth; also, note the relative size of tubercles on the eyelid, lower lip, and limbs. The frog was found on a leaf during the night (left photograph) and photographed in the laboratory (photograph with white background) the following morning.
Figure 5. Oscillogram and spectrogram for the three call types A–C, of Pristimantis mutabilis on the same time scale. Note that call type C is a series of calls.
Figure 6. Map showing the distribution of Pristimantis mutabilis (white circles) in Ecuador.

A, larva, dorsal; B, head, dorsal; C, same, ventral; D, labrum, dorsal; E, right mandible, ventral; F, left mandible, ventral; G, right pronotal plate, dorsal; H, right pleura and legs on thorax, right lateral; I, dorsal tergite on segment IX, dorsal; J, right anal leg, right lateral.

Early instar larva (A–G): A, 4th instar larva, right, lateral; B–D, right lateral parts of abdominal segment II of 4th (B), 3rd (C) and 2nd (D) instar larvae, dorsal; E–G, dorsal tergites of segment IX of 4th (E), 3rd (F) and 2nd (G) instar larvae, dorsal. Eggs (H and inset): eggs on liverwort. Case (I), dorsal. Feeding trace (J): cells of the liverwort showing feeding trace, see text.

FIGURE 6. Occurrence of 5 development stages of Palaeagapetus nearcticus in British Colombia (B), Washington (W), Mary’s Peak in Oregon (M), other localities in Oregon (O), and California (C) in 1948–2012. Grey bands indicate the supposed main annual life cycle in all study sites.

FIGURE 1. Palaeagapetus nearcticus male and female. Male (A–I): A, head, left lateral; B, head and thorax, dorsal; C, right wings, dorsal; D, abdominal segments V–X, left lateral; E, genitalia, left lateral; F, same, dorsal; G, same, ventral; H, I, lateral appendage, left lateral. Female (J, K): J, abdominal segments V–X, left lateral; K, vaginal apparatus, ventral. Abbreviations: 1–5=1st to 5th apical forks; V–X= abdominal segments V–X; tX= tergite X. Setal warts (B): a= anterior setal wart, am= anteromesal setal wart, p=posterior setal wart, pl=posterolateral setal wart. Genitalia: ia=inferior appendage (paired): dl=dorsal lobe of inferior appendage, vl=ventral lobe of inferior appendage; la ap=lateral appendage (paired): db=dorsal branch of lateral appendage; mb=middle branch of lateral appendage, vb=ventral branch of lateral appendage; ph, phallus.

treatments.articleTitle: A new frog species from rapidly dwindling cloud forest streams of Sri Lanka-Lankanectes pera (Anura, Nyctibatrachidae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Senevirathne, Gayani, Samarawickrama, V. A. M. P. K., Wijayathilaka, Nayana, Manamendra-Arachchi, Kelum, Bowatte, Gayan, Samarawickrama, D. R. N. S. & Meegaskumbura, Madhava

images.captionText: FIGURE 2. Lankanectes pera sp. nov. in life, SVL 22.40 mm: (A) Dorsolateral view; (B) Dorsal view; (C) Habitat—clear water stream habitat under montane forest canopy cover.

FIGURE 3. Illustrations of (A) Lankanectes corrugatus, ZMB4897, mature male, SVL 43.70 mm and (B) L. pera sp. nov., DZ1858, mature male, SVL 66.0 mm, anal region (top); manus (middle) and maxilla (lower).

FIGURE 4. Lankanectes pera sp. nov., SVL 68.69 mm, DZ1307 (A–D), in preservation. (A) ventral view; (B) right ventral foot; (C) ventral view of the head; (D) lateral view. Lankanectes corrugatus, SVL 55.28 mm, DZ1396 (E–H), in preservation. (A) ventral view; (B) right ventral foot; (C) ventral view of the head; (D) lateral view.

FIGURE 1. (A) 16S rRNA Maximum-likelihood phylogram for 41 taxa with node support (posterior probabilities above nodes, with values>95 indicated by a *; MP bootstrap values below nodes, with values>50) showing two clearly-defined clades including L. corrugatus and L. pera (B) The uncorrected pairwise genetic distances range for 16S rRNA 3.5–3.7%; Haplotype network analysis using the full dataset with 485 bp, shows all haplotypes shared across L. corrugatus; L. pera sp. nov. is different by more than 16 mutational steps from the L. corrugatus populations. (C) The predicted distributions for L. corrugatus is 360 km 2 and L. pera sp. nov. is 14120 km 2 on the northern hills of Sri Lanka. (D) Component loadings of the first two principal component axes (PC1 vs PC2) shows clear separation of the males, but with slight overlap of females; L. pera sp. nov. indicated in red, L. corrugatus in blue (filled circles denote females and open circles denote males).
